content.text: Deprecated Prompt Grace Periods and Support

A grace period is the window of time after the official sunset date during which a deprecated prompt version continues to function but receives only minimal support. Grace periods exist because not every caller can complete a migration before the sunset date even with adequate advance notice: a team may be in the middle of an unrelated critical project, the migration may have taken longer than estimated, or an architecture migration may have surfaced unexpected complexity. Rather than forcing an all-or-nothing choice between hard retirement on the sunset date and indefinite extensions, the grace period provides a predictable, time-bounded continuation that gives genuinely constrained teams enough runway while preserving the team's ability to retire the deprecated version on a defined schedule.

The distinction between a sunset date and a grace period end date is important: the sunset date is the point at which the deprecated version is no longer actively maintained, new callers are blocked, and the migration is officially overdue. The grace period end date is the point at which the deprecated version is actually retired. This two-date model lets the owner team make a clean public commitment at T-90 (the sunset date) without needing to accommodate every possible caller constraint indefinitely, while still avoiding the operational disruption of forcing a hard cutoff on a single date without any flexibility.

attrs.alt: Grace period policy by migration track: Drop-in 30 days, Prompt Adaptation 60 days, Architecture Migration 90 days, each with support level and post-grace outcome

content.text: The grace period length is calibrated to the migration track, not to caller preference. A Drop-in Replacement migration requires a version pin swap and a regression test run — a team that has not completed this in 90 days of advance notice should be able to do it in the 30-day grace period. A Prompt Adaptation migration requires prompt edits and eval validation — 60 days of grace accommodates teams that started late but are genuinely progressing. An Architecture Migration may require a complete multi-step redesign — 90 days of grace acknowledges that such work cannot be fully completed even with a 180-day advance notice window if other priorities intervened, but makes clear that the support commitment ends.

The deprecated prompt version transitions through a series of states as the sunset and grace period milestones pass. Modelling this as an explicit state machine makes the policy enforceable in code and prevents edge cases like a version that is simultaneously 'in sunset warning' and 'in grace period' being treated as if it were still actively supported.

The support scope defines what the team maintaining the deprecated version will and will not do during the grace period. Without an explicit scope, the implicit expectation is that the deprecated version receives the same level of attention as the active version, which is neither operationally sustainable nor appropriate when the team's migration-focused attention should be on the replacement version. Making the scope explicit at announcement time sets caller expectations correctly and prevents support requests for non-security bugs in the grace period from creating unplanned work.
The three support tiers apply sequentially as the version ages through its lifecycle:
All bugs, security, performance, new features on request
Performance improvements, new features, minor bugs
Grace Period Monitoring and Caller Communication

During the grace period, the team must actively monitor which callers are still on the deprecated version and communicate the approaching cutoff to them with increasing urgency. The monitoring model below tracks the grace period state for each active sunset notice and generates the appropriate communications at each milestone. Passive monitoring (waiting for callers to reach out) is insufficient — callers that missed the original sunset notice may not be aware that they are in a grace period, let alone that it is about to end.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List
import datetime


@dataclass
class GracePeriodMilestone:
    days_before_end: int
    action: str
    channel: str   # "email" | "dashboard" | "api-header" | "direct"


STANDARD_GRACE_MILESTONES: List[GracePeriodMilestone] = [
    GracePeriodMilestone(
        days_before_end=60,
        action="Send grace period start notice to all callers still on deprecated version",
        channel="email",
    ),
    GracePeriodMilestone(
        days_before_end=30,
        action="Update dashboard to show GRACE PERIOD warning; send targeted email to non-migrated callers",
        channel="dashboard",
    ),
    GracePeriodMilestone(
        days_before_end=14,
        action="Direct outreach to tech-leads of callers with > 5% traffic on deprecated version",
        channel="direct",
    ),
    GracePeriodMilestone(
        days_before_end=7,
        action="Daily summary email to caller contacts; add X-Grace-Period-Ends header to all responses",
        channel="api-header",
    ),
    GracePeriodMilestone(
        days_before_end=1,
        action="Final notification: grace ends tomorrow; ensure on-call is aware of retirement",
        channel="email",
    ),
]


@dataclass
class GracePeriodCommunicationPlan:
    notice_id: str
    grace_end_date: str   # ISO-8601
    milestones: List[GracePeriodMilestone] = field(
        default_factory=lambda: list(STANDARD_GRACE_MILESTONES)
    )

    def overdue_actions(self) -> List[GracePeriodMilestone]:
        """Milestones whose communication should already have been sent."""
        grace_end = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.grace_end_date)
        today = datetime.date.today()
        return [
            m for m in self.milestones
            if today >= grace_end - datetime.timedelta(days=m.days_before_end)
        ]

    def next_action(self) -> GracePeriodMilestone | None:
        """The next milestone that has not yet been triggered."""
        grace_end = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.grace_end_date)
        today = datetime.date.today()
        upcoming = [
            m for m in sorted(self.milestones, key=lambda x: x.days_before_end, reverse=True)
            if today < grace_end - datetime.timedelta(days=m.days_before_end)
        ]
        return upcoming[-1] if upcoming else None

    def communication_status(self) -> str:
        overdue = self.overdue_actions()
        nxt = self.next_action()
        grace_end = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.grace_end_date)
        days_remaining = (grace_end - datetime.date.today()).days
        lines = [
            f"Grace period plan for {self.notice_id} (ends {self.grace_end_date}, {days_remaining}d remaining)",
            f"Actions due: {len(overdue)} of {len(self.milestones)}",
        ]
        if nxt:
            trigger_date = grace_end - datetime.timedelta(days=nxt.days_before_end)
            lines.append(f"Next: {nxt.action} (trigger date {trigger_date.isoformat()}, via {nxt.channel})")
        else:
            lines.append("All milestones passed. Grace period ends imminently.")
        return "\n".join(lines)

Despite the monitoring and communication plan, some callers will reach the grace period end date without completing their migration. The post-grace handling policy determines what happens to those callers and how the team responds. The policy must be defined before the grace period starts — not decided ad hoc when callers surface after the retirement date — to avoid creating inconsistent precedents that undermine future sunset processes.

Grace periods provide a predictable, time-bounded accommodation for callers that cannot complete their migration before the sunset date, without allowing the deprecated version to persist indefinitely. The grace period length is calibrated to the migration track (30 days for Drop-in Replacement, 60 days for Prompt Adaptation, 90 days for Architecture Migration), the support scope during grace is limited to security patches only, and communication milestones ensure that callers receive increasing-urgency notifications as the grace end date approaches. Post-grace callers that have not migrated face a hard retirement, with emergency grace available only for callers who are genuinely in the final days of their migration.
Announce the grace period end date alongside the sunset date at T-90; callers should plan to the grace end date, not just the sunset date.
Reduce the support scope to security patches only from the sunset date onward — do not fix bugs in the deprecated version during the grace period.
Send proactive communications at 60, 30, 14, and 7 days before the grace end date; do not rely on callers to monitor the dashboard.
Define the post-grace policy (hard retirement, auto-redirect, emergency grace criteria) before the grace period starts, not when the first post-grace caller surfaces.
Emergency grace extensions must be capped at two sprints and must require a written migration completion commitment from the caller's tech lead.
